text: The robotic-assisted adrenalectomy begins with dissection around the left renal vein, a large blue structure visible below the scissors. The surgeon searches for the solitary adrenal vein, which is identified and clipped using a robotic clip applier to free the adrenal gland from the kidney vasculature.

The surgeon places additional clips on the adrenal vein before cutting through it, exposing the area behind the adrenal gland. Several lymphatic vessels and small veins are visible, managed with cautery. The adrenal gland is dissected from surrounding structures, including the kidney below and the pancreas and spleen above, with magnification highlighting small vessels.

The surgeon performs dissection at the top part of the surgical field, carefully separating the adrenal lesion from surrounding retroperitoneal structures. A large splenic vessel is visible near the dissection site, and additional dissection is conducted close to the aorta, with visible pulsations affecting nearby tissues. Clips are applied to vascular structures as needed to secure them during the procedure.

The surgeon dissects around the kidney, revealing the kidney capsule and some Gerota's fascia. A small artery, identified as an upper pole branch of the kidney, is visible and preserved during the procedure. Below the artery, a vein is also visible. The surgeon carefully manages unnamed posterior perforating veins, ensuring their preservation during the left adrenalectomy.

The robotic-assisted dissection reveals the adrenal gland being separated from the kidney, with visible manipulation of the Girotis fascia. The surgeon uses magnification to carefully navigate the tissue planes, noting inflammation on the kidney due to the patient's history of lithotripsy. Assistants provide retraction to enhance visibility during the procedure.

The surgeon completes the dissection around the pancreas and spleen, fully freeing the adrenal gland and its lesion. The adrenal mass, visible as a yellowish round structure on the left side of the screen, is surrounded by fat and fascia. The mass is placed in a bag and removed through a twelve millimeter port.

The surgeon inspects the clipped veins and vessels to ensure they are secure, applying a hemostatic agent, Flowseal, to the area. The bowels, spleen, and pancreas are repositioned to their normal anatomical locations, with no drain left in place.
